footer.description: An essential guide to reading the glacial landscape of Antigorio Valley, one step at a time.
footer.disclaimer: Always check trail conditions, weather and local information before visiting.

meta.title: Uriezzo Gorges and the Giant’s Potholes
meta.description: Explore two glacial landscapes in Antigorio Valley with practical guides, multilingual audio and visitor information.

home.title: Two landscapes carved by water
home.intro: Between granite walls, damp woodland and the Toce River, the trail tells a story of ice, whirlpools and rock spanning thousands of years. Choose where to begin.
home.trailNote: The South Gorge and the Maiesso potholes are connected, so both can be visited on the same walk when time and conditions allow.

meta.title: Uriezzo Gorges
meta.description: Guide to the Uriezzo Gorges: glacial geology, access from Premia, Baceno and Verampio, safety and visiting tips.

orridi.heroAlt: Deep alpine gorge between rock walls, illustrative image
orridi.title: Uriezzo Gorges
orridi.subtitle: A canyon without a river, where the glacier left granite corridors, shade and silence.
intro: Entering the gorges means walking where streams once ran beneath the Toce Glacier. Water no longer fills these passages; polished walls, sudden bends and a narrow blade of sky now guide the route.

Moisture sustains mosses and ferns while light filters down and changes tone across the rock. Sometimes called the “Grand Canyon of Piedmont”, it is a more intimate landscape whose scale is best understood on foot.

arrival.title: How to get there
arrival.body: The main access points are Premia, Baceno and Verampio. Premia is often the most convenient. From Baceno, park near the Church of San Gaudenzio and follow the signs and yellow arrows on foot. Always rely on current on-site signage as well, since mobile coverage can be uneven.

geology.title: History and geology
geology.body: During the last glaciation, which ended roughly 12,000 years ago, subglacial streams flowed beneath the Toce Glacier. Water, pressure and abrasive debris cut into the granite. When the ice retreated, the streams dried up and left fossil gorges that can now be walked through.

visit.title: Visiting advice
visit.body: Wear footwear with good grip: the floor may be wet and some sections include metal ladders. Avoid flip-flops and winter visits, when falling ice can be hazardous. Spring and autumn are generally preferable; wild cherry trees may flower from late March into April.

highlights.title: Each gorge has a different character
items: South Gorge, locally called “Tomba d’Uriezzo”: the most dramatic, about 200 metres long and up to 20–30 metres deep.
North-East Gorge: about 100 metres long, narrower and more enclosed, with walls around 10 metres high.
West Gorge: less monumental and better suited to experienced hikers; a rope assists some passages.
Vallaccia: a fourth, difficult-to-reach gorge not recommended for an ordinary tourist visit.

gallery.title: Rock, shade and vegetation
gallery.intro: These are alpine-themed placeholders. Replace them with authorised photographs of the site before publication.

items.alt: Rock walls in an alpine gorge
items.caption: Rock corridors shaped by water.
Mosses and ferns thrive in the shade.
Water continues to shape the land.
Access routes and links are walked.
Suitable footwear and a careful pace.

orridi.narration: The Uriezzo Gorges lie in Antigorio Valley near Baceno, Premia and Verampio. They were carved into granite by streams flowing beneath the Toce Glacier during the last glaciation, which ended about twelve thousand years ago. When the ice retreated, the streams dried up and left a fossil canyon that can now be explored on foot. The South Gorge, locally known as Tomba d’Uriezzo, is the most spectacular. It is about two hundred metres long and between twenty and thirty metres deep. The North-East Gorge is shorter and narrower, while the West Gorge requires more experience. Spring and autumn are the best seasons. Shoes with good grip are essential because the floor can be damp and some sections include metal ladders. Winter visits are discouraged because of the risk of falling ice.

meta.title: Giant’s Potholes at Maiesso
meta.description: Guide to the Giant’s Potholes on the Toce: glacial formation, link to the gorges, access and visitor advice.

marmitte.heroAlt: Alpine water among large polished rocks, illustrative image
marmitte.title: Giant’s Potholes
marmitte.subtitle: Ancient whirlpools, pebbles and meltwater transformed granite into almost perfect forms.
intro: At Maiesso, the Toce reveals the slow, powerful work of water. Hemispherical and cylindrical hollows appear in the rock, polished like enormous natural vessels: the Giant’s Potholes.

Their name suggests legends, but their origin is geological. Swirling currents loaded with sand and pebbles rotated in the same places, wearing down granite over millennia and leaving surfaces that look hand carved.

facts.label: From Baceno
facts.value: about 1 h 10 min return with gorges

arrival.title: How to get there
arrival.body: The potholes lie at Maiesso beside the Toce. From the gorges trail, continue beyond the South Gorge towards Maiesso Bridge to combine both sites. From Baceno, the full return route takes roughly one hour and ten minutes, although stops and walking pace can add time.

geology.title: History and geology
geology.body: Glacial meltwater once flowed with great energy. Sand and pebbles caught in whirlpools acted as abrasive tools, rotating and cutting progressively deeper hollows. The rounded shapes preserve evidence of river dynamics and the valley’s glacial past.

visit.title: Visiting advice
visit.body: Stay on safe passages and keep away from wet rocks and the river edge. Polished granite can be extremely slippery and the Toce’s flow may change. Wear hiking shoes, supervise children and turn back during high water, storms or any locally announced closure.

highlights.title: What to notice on site
items: Rounded hollows, some hemispherical and others more cylindrical, cut by sediment-filled whirlpools.
Smooth, grooved granite surfaces showing repeated abrasion by sand and pebbles.
The relationship between the potholes, Maiesso Bridge and the exit of the South Gorge along one route.
The nearby Crego hydroelectric station, designed by Piero Portaluppi between 1917 and 1926, as an optional industrial-history detail.

gallery.title: Water, granite and woodland
gallery.intro: These river and alpine images are placeholders. Replace them with authorised photographs from Maiesso.

items.alt: Water and mountains in an alpine valley
items.caption: The Toce links water, ice and landscape.
The valley’s forms arise from its glacial past.
The trail passes through a damp environment.
Life settles even on the hardest surfaces.
Observe details without leaving the route.

marmitte.narration: The Giant’s Potholes are at Maiesso beside the Toce River and are connected to the Uriezzo Gorges by the same trail. Continuing beyond the South Gorge leads to Maiesso Bridge and then to the potholes. These hemispherical or cylindrical forms were cut into the rock by powerful glacial meltwater. Sand and pebbles carried by the current became trapped in whirlpools and acted as abrasive tools, wearing away the granite over thousands of years. From Baceno, a return route including both the gorges and the potholes takes roughly one hour and ten minutes without long stops. Wet rock can be extremely slippery and the Toce’s flow can vary. Stay on safe passages, wear suitable shoes and do not visit during high water or storms. Nearby stands the Crego hydroelectric station, designed by Piero Portaluppi between 1917 and 1926.
